title: What Is Artificial Intelligence?
obj: Students will be able to define artificial intelligence, distinguish it from ordinary software, and reflect on the current state of the field.
content: Definition
AI = computer systems that perform tasks normally requiring human intelligence: perception, reasoning, learning, decision-making, language
Narrow vs general AI
narrow (task-specific, today's reality) vs general (human-level across domains, research goal) vs super (speculative)
How AI differs from normal software
rules are learned from data rather than hand-coded; systems adapt and improve with more data
Current state (2026)
large language models, image/video generation, autonomous systems, AI copilots in every industry
Limits of current AI
brittle outside training data, hallucinations, high compute cost, bias inherited from data, no true understanding
assess: Short answer: write a 3-sentence definition of AI in your own words
Classify 6 example systems (calculator, Netflix recommender, thermostat, ChatGPT, self-driving car, spell-check) as 'AI' / 'not AI' / 'borderline' with justification
Reflection: name one AI tool you used this week and what it did for you

title: History and Evolution of AI
obj: Students will be able to describe key eras and milestones in AI history and identify important early contributors.
content: 1950s origins
Turing Test (1950), Dartmouth Workshop (1956) where the term 'AI' was coined, early optimism
Symbolic AI era (1960s-70s)
rule-based expert systems, ELIZA chatbot, logic theorem-provers; first AI Winter late 70s
Knowledge-based era (1980s)
expert systems in industry; second AI Winter late 80s when promises exceeded delivery
Machine-learning era (1990s-2010s)
statistical methods, IBM Deep Blue beats Kasparov (1997), IBM Watson wins Jeopardy (2011)
Deep-learning + LLM era (2012-now)
AlexNet (2012), AlphaGo (2016), GPT-3 (2020), ChatGPT (2022), multimodal GenAI (2024+)
Key contributors
Alan Turing, John McCarthy, Marvin Minsky, Geoffrey Hinton, Yann LeCun, Yoshua Bengio, Fei-Fei Li
assess: Timeline exercise: place 8 events on a timeline labeled 1950-2026
Match 5 contributors to their signature contributions
Short answer: what caused the 'AI Winters' and what ended them?

title: How AI Has Solved Problems — Past, Present, Future
obj: Students will be able to describe how AI has been used to solve problems historically and identify current and near-future problem areas where AI delivers value.
content: Historical wins
chess/Go (game-playing), speech-to-text, OCR on mail, ad targeting, fraud detection
Current wins
medical image diagnosis, drug discovery, protein folding (AlphaFold), language translation, code assistance
Near-term wins
autonomous delivery, personalized tutoring, climate modeling, early-warning systems
Where AI still fails
common-sense reasoning, novel situations, small-data problems, tasks requiring real understanding
Pattern
AI excels at narrow, data-rich tasks with clear success criteria; struggles with broad, sparse-data tasks
assess: Group the 8 example problems by era (past/present/future) and justify
Short answer: why has AI succeeded at image recognition but struggles with common-sense reasoning?
Design: propose an AI solution for a problem in your school or community

title: Major AI Domains: ML, NLP, and Computer Vision
obj: Students will be able to define machine learning, natural language processing, and computer vision, and identify which domain applies to a given example.
content: Machine learning (ML)
algorithms that learn patterns from data rather than being programmed with rules; the engine behind most modern AI
Natural language processing (NLP)
AI working with human language: translation, sentiment, summarization, chatbots, ChatGPT-style generation
Computer vision (CV)
AI that interprets images and video: face detection, self-driving perception, X-ray analysis, OCR
Speech AI
speech-to-text, text-to-speech, voice cloning — a sub-area sitting between NLP and audio signal processing
Reinforcement learning
agents that learn from rewards and penalties (used in robotics, game-playing, recommendation)
Generative AI
cross-cuts all domains — produces new text, images, audio, code from prompts
assess: Classify 10 everyday AI examples into ML / NLP / CV / Speech / RL / GenAI buckets
Short answer: why is 'machine learning' a foundational concept that NLP and CV both rely on?
Research: find one current product in each of ML, NLP, CV and describe what it does

title: AI You Interact With Every Day
obj: Students will be able to identify AI in common apps and services and explain what problem each AI is intended to solve.
content: Social media
feed ranking, content moderation, friend suggestions, auto-captioning, deepfake detection
Messaging and email
spam filtering, smart replies, autocomplete, autocorrect, voice-to-text
Shopping and media
product recommendations, price prediction, Netflix/Spotify rankings, personalized ads
Navigation and travel
route optimization (Google Maps), rideshare matching, flight-price forecasting
Assistants
Siri, Alexa, Google Assistant, Copilot — voice + intent understanding
Camera and photos
portrait mode, scene recognition, photo search by content, object erasure
assess: Audit your phone: list 10 apps and note which use AI and for what
Analyze: pick 3 services and describe what problem the AI solves vs. what manual task it replaced
Short answer: how does a recommender system differ from a simple 'most popular' list?

title: AI and the Future of Work
obj: Students will be able to discuss how AI is reshaping careers, identify new AI-adjacent jobs, and analyze how existing roles are shifting.
content: Jobs AI creates
prompt engineer, ML engineer, data scientist, AI ethicist, AI auditor, AI product manager, AI trainer
Jobs AI transforms
writers use AI drafting tools, teachers use AI tutoring aides, designers use AI generators as co-pilots
Tasks AI automates
routine document review, first-pass customer support, image tagging, basic coding assistance, scheduling
Skills that stay valuable
judgment, domain expertise, creativity, relationship-building, complex problem-solving, ethical reasoning
Preparing now
learn to direct AI (prompting), evaluate AI output critically, build portfolios that show your AI use
assess: Research: find one career you're interested in — describe 3 ways AI is changing how that work gets done
Design: plan a 3-year learning path to enter an AI-adjacent role
Debate: 'AI will eliminate more jobs than it creates' — argue both sides in 3 sentences each

title: AI's Impact on Art and Creative Industries
obj: Students will be able to identify how AI is used in creative fields and analyze the ethical debates around AI-generated art.
content: Visual art
Midjourney, DALL-E, Stable Diffusion — text-to-image; also style transfer and image editing tools
Music
Suno/Udio for composition, AI mastering tools, voice cloning for vocalists, royalty-free track generation
Writing and film
AI screenplay assist, video generation (Sora, Runway), automated editing, color grading
Ethical debates
copyright on training data, style mimicry, artist consent, authenticity, attribution
Artists' response
workflow integration, new art forms (prompt art), guild pushback, licensing frameworks
assess: Experiment: use a free AI image generator to create 3 variations on a theme; compare results
Analyze: find 2 news stories about AI and creative industries — summarize the positions
Short answer: is AI-generated art 'real' art? Defend your position in 3 sentences

title: Training Your First ML Model with Teachable Machine
obj: Students will be able to use a no-code web tool (Google Teachable Machine or Weka) to train a simple image classifier and evaluate its accuracy.
content: Tool overview
Google Teachable Machine — browser-based, no-code ML for image/sound/pose classification
Gather samples
use webcam or uploaded images to create at least two classes (e.g., happy vs. sad faces)
Train the model
click 'Train Model' — the tool uses transfer learning from MobileNet; takes ~30 seconds
Test predictions
preview live predictions; see confidence scores; identify misclassifications
Export and share
download the model as TensorFlow.js, TFLite, or keras; embed in a webpage or Python
Iterate
add more training samples for weak classes; balance the dataset; retest
assess: Hands-on: build and train a 2-class image classifier (e.g., with/without glasses); achieve >80% test accuracy
Document: write a 1-paragraph reflection on what surprised you about training
Troubleshoot: identify one type of example your model got wrong and hypothesize why

title: Tracking Current AI Research and Industry Events
obj: Students will be able to find, read at a high level, and summarize current AI research and industry developments.
content: Research sources
arXiv.org (preprints), Papers With Code, major conferences: NeurIPS, ICML, CVPR, ACL
Industry news
TechCrunch AI, Ars Technica, The Batch (Andrew Ng), Import AI newsletter, MIT Tech Review
Reading a paper
abstract first, then intro + conclusion, then skim methods; don't get bogged down in math early
Evaluating credibility
peer review vs preprint, source reputation, reproducibility, independent replication
Tracking trends
follow specific researchers on social media; subscribe to weekly AI newsletters; set up Google Alerts
assess: Find a recent AI paper on arXiv in a topic you care about; summarize the abstract in plain English
Compare: find the same AI news story covered in 2 different sources; note tone and framing differences
Curate: build a list of 5 sources you'll follow weekly for AI news

title: Current and Predicted AI Industry Trends
obj: Students will be able to identify current trends in the AI industry and make informed predictions about near-term developments.
content: Current investment trends
massive capital into foundation-model companies, AI chip (GPU/TPU) shortages, enterprise AI adoption
Regulatory trends
EU AI Act in force, US executive orders and state laws, GDPR + privacy intersections
Technical trends
multimodal models, smaller efficient models (distilled), on-device AI, agents, reasoning models
Workforce trends
mass upskilling, AI literacy in every role, shift from 'AI teams' to 'AI everywhere'
Risks on the radar
disinformation, copyright lawsuits, energy cost, job displacement, concentration of power
assess: Research: name 3 companies driving AI investment right now and what each is betting on
Predict: write 3 predictions for the next 12 months with your reasoning
Short answer: what regulatory change do you think will have the biggest impact on students entering AI?

title: AI Beyond Machine Learning
obj: Students will be able to describe AI approaches that don't rely on machine learning — genetic algorithms, symbolic AI, rule-based systems, and classical robotics.
content: Genetic algorithms
evolutionary optimization — initial population, fitness scoring, crossover, mutation, repeat
Symbolic AI / expert systems
encoded human expertise as if-then rules; still used in tax software, medical triage, regulatory compliance
Search algorithms
A*, minimax with alpha-beta pruning, constraint satisfaction — how game-playing and routing work
Classical robotics
inverse kinematics, PID controllers, sensor fusion — the non-learned math of robot motion
Hybrid systems
modern AI often combines ML with symbolic rules (e.g., safety constraints on an LLM output)
assess: Identify: given 5 scenarios, say whether ML, symbolic AI, or a hybrid fits best and why
Research: find one production system that uses a genetic algorithm or classical search
Short answer: why hasn't ML replaced symbolic AI in all applications?

title: Making Informed Predictions About AI's Future
obj: Students will be able to apply structured forecasting methods to make defensible predictions about AI developments.
content: Forecasting methods
extrapolate current trends; reference class forecasting; scenario planning; Delphi panels
Base rates
how long have similar technologies taken to reach 10M / 100M / 1B users? use these as baselines
Driver analysis
what forces accelerate (compute, capital, talent) vs. slow (regulation, energy, trust) a given prediction
Calibration
track your predictions with confidence intervals; review and update as evidence comes in
Avoiding pitfalls
beware of confident-sounding timelines; watch for motivated reasoning; diversify your sources
assess: Make 3 predictions for 2027 with explicit probabilities (e.g., '70% chance X happens')
Identify one prediction from 2020-2022 about AI that aged well and one that aged poorly; explain why
Design: build a one-page scenario for 'the best-case 2030 AI world' and 'the worst-case'
